title: Roo
text:
Roo
    soft mind, sharp thinking

    
      ☁️ fintech
      🔍 risk & aml
      👀 data stories
      ✨ tech + aesthetics
    

    scroll to explore ↓
  





  
    Hello
    I'm Roopa — Roo.
    
      Masters student at SMU, with an interest in
      understanding financial crime         and studying the intersection of technology,finance, and design.
    
    
      I like to build things that are impactful and beautiful. Data tells us many          stories, it is a matter of perspective and that is what I like to explore.
    
  





  Independent Work · Fintech & Financial Crime
  Projects

  
    Self-directed explorations across AML typologies, risk analytics, and emerging
    areas in financial crime — built to go beyond the classroom and demonstrate
    genuine curiosity about where the field is heading.
  
  
  

    
      🎨
      Art Markets & the PEP Problem
      
        The art market's opacity makes it a documented vehicle for laundering — 
        especially among politically exposed persons. This project combines auction 
        data, ICIJ offshore leaks, and PEP network analysis to visualise how money 
        and influence move through high-value cultural assets.
      
      R · Tableau · ICIJ · AML
    

    
      🚢
      Trade-Based Money Laundering
      
        TBML is one of the most significant and least visible ML typologies — 
        manipulating trade invoices to move value across borders. Using UN Comtrade 
        mirror trade data, this project surfaces discrepancies across high-risk 
        corridors and maps them to FATF and MAS typology frameworks.
      
      Python · UN Comtrade · FATF
    

    
      🔗
      Crypto & Financial Crime
      
        An honest exploration of on-chain analytics and the evolving regulatory 
        landscape for virtual assets. Part research, part hands-on — tracing 
        transaction flows, understanding mixer patterns, and mapping where 
        MAS and FATF guidance currently stands.
      
      Python · Etherscan · Research

title: Crypto & Financial Crime
text:
Exploration · Research & Hands-On · Crypto AML
    Crypto & Financial Crime
    
      An honest, in-progress exploration of on-chain analytics and the evolving regulatory
      landscape for virtual assets. Part research, part hands-on — tracing transaction
      flows on public blockchains, understanding how mixing and layering work in practice,
      and mapping where MAS and FATF guidance currently stands and where it doesn't.
    
  

  
    Python
    Etherscan API
    On-Chain Analytics
    MAS Guidelines
    FATF Virtual Assets
    Ongoing ✦
  

  

  Why this, and why this framing
  
    Crypto compliance is the fastest-growing area in financial crime, and MAS is actively
    building its regulatory framework for digital payment token service providers under
    the Payment Services Act. But it is also genuinely complex — the technology moves
    faster than regulation, the terminology is contested, and a lot of what's written
    about it is either too technical or too superficial. This is an exploration, not a
    finished project. The framing matters: I'd rather show the process of learning
    something rigorously than pretend to expertise I don't yet have.
  

  Research thread
  
    The written component maps the current regulatory landscape: FATF's updated virtual
    asset guidance (2021, revised 2023), the travel rule and its implementation challenges
    across jurisdictions, MAS's Notice PSN02 and how Singapore's framework compares to
    the EU's MiCA. It also covers the core AML typologies specific to virtual assets —
    mixer and tumbler usage, chain-hopping, peer-to-peer networks, and DeFi as a
    layering vehicle.
  

  Hands-on thread
  
    Using the Etherscan API and Elliptic's publicly released academic dataset (which
    contains labelled illicit and licit wallet addresses), the practical component traces
    transaction flows around known case studies — the Bitfinex hack of 2016 is fully
    documented on-chain and makes an instructive case study for how large-scale layering
    actually moves through a public ledger. The goal is a simple flow visualisation and
    hop analysis, not a predictive model. Seeing the data is the point.
  

  
    
      🔗
      Etherscan API
      Public Ethereum transaction data — wallet balances, transfer histories, contract interactions.
    
    
      📊
      Elliptic Academic Dataset
      Labelled Bitcoin transaction graph with known illicit and licit classifications — used in academic research on on-chain anomaly detection.
    
    
      📑
      MAS / FATF Guidance
      Primary source regulatory documents — FATF virtual asset guidance, MAS PSN02, Payment Services Act — forming the research backbone.
    
  

  
    ✦ This is a living exploration — research notes, code, and findings will be added here as the work develops.
